qa_pairs.question: How do users enable and utilize a compiled User-Defined Function (UDF) for Ansys Fluent simulations on an Alliance cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: To use a compiled UDF, it must first be compiled on an Alliance cluster. This process creates a `libudf` subdirectory containing the necessary `libudf.so` shared library. The `libudf` directory cannot simply be copied from a remote system due to library dependencies, but it can be transferred between Alliance clusters if your account loads the same `StdEnv` environment module version. In your journal file, uncomment the line `define/user-defined/compiled-functions load libudf` to use the compiled UDF. It's important not to uncomment both the compile and load commands in the journal file, as this leads to inefficient recompilation for every job and potential build conflicts. Alternatively, you can compile the UDF using the Fluent GUI by navigating to the Compiled UDFs Dialog Box, adding the source file, and clicking 'Build'.

qa_pairs.question: What considerations are important for using a User-Defined Function (UDF) with parallel Ansys Fluent jobs on an Alliance cluster?
qa_pairs.answer: For a User-Defined Function (UDF) to work effectively with parallel Ansys Fluent jobs, including both single-node Shared Memory Parallel (SMP) and multinode Message Passing Interface (MPI) configurations, it must be parallelized. This parallelization involves adding compiler directives, predicates, and reduction macros into your existing serial UDF code. Failure to properly parallelize the UDF can result in slow performance or immediate crashes during execution. Detailed information on this process can be found in `Part I: Chapter 7: Parallel Considerations` of the Ansys 2024 `Fluent Customization Manual`.
